summary:
The tyranny of Peisistratos (c. 561–527 BC, with interruptions) and his sons (Hippias and Hipparchus, 527–510 BC) was a period of significant urban development, cultural patronage, and Athenian consolidation that paradoxically laid groundwork for subsequent democracy. Peisistratos came to power through populist politics — he reportedly staged a theatrical trick, arriving in Athens with a tall woman dressed as Athena and claiming divine sponsorship — and maintained himself through cleverness, economic development, and popular support among the rural poor.

Peisistratos's rule was in many ways constructive: he organized the [[event:1986|Panathenaic Games]], patronized the arts and literature (the commissioning and standardization of the Homeric texts is attributed to his court), constructed public fountains and temples, and promoted trade that enriched Athens. His seizure of the silver mines of Laurion and control of the Hellespont established Athenian commercial interests that would dominate for generations. His sons' rule was more repressive; after Hipparchus was assassinated by Harmodius and Aristogeiton (514 BC), Hippias became tyrannical, was expelled with Spartan help in 510 BC, and fled to Persia — where he guided the Persian force at Marathon in 490 BC, seeking restoration.
